title: Rethinking Expressibility-Trainability Trade-off in Hybrid Quantum Neural Networks
abstract: Hybrid quantum neural networks (HQNNs) integrate parameterized quantum circuits (PQCs) within classical networks, where the behavior of the underlying PQCs is often the primary focus of analysis. In this context, expressibility and trainability are widely used to characterize PQC's performance and are commonly assumed to exhibit a trade-off, where highly expressive circuits are more susceptible to barren plateaus. However, the validity of this relationship in HQNNs remains unclear. In this paper, we systematically analyze the expressibility--trainability relationship in HQNNs across varying circuit depths, qubit counts, entanglement topologies. We consider different training configurations, including pure PQCs, quantum-only training in hybrid setting, and full end-to-end training of hybrid models. Our results show that pure PQCs exhibit only a weak and regime-dependent trade-off, while hybrid architectures increasingly disrupt and can eliminate this relationship under full hybrid training. This indicates that classical components reshape the optimization landscape, decoupling trainability from PQC expressibility. We further propose a multi-objective neural architecture search (NAS) framework that jointly optimizes expressibility, trainability, and task performance over a combined classical--quantum design space, revealing different Pareto-optimal solutions under full end-to-end and quantum only training in hybrid setting. different trainability definitions. Our results suggest that hybridization is not just an implementation detail, but a defining factor in the performance of quantum machine learning models.
summary: This paper demonstrates that the integration of classical layers in hybrid quantum neural networks fundamentally changes the optimization landscape, breaking the traditional trade-off between circuit expressibility and trainability. By using a multi-objective neural architecture search, the authors show that classical components can mitigate barren plateaus, suggesting that hybrid design principles differ significantly from standalone quantum circuit design.
main_problem: Investigating whether the well-known expressibility-trainability trade-off in standalone parameterized quantum circuits (PQCs) persists in hybrid quantum-classical neural networks (HQNNs).
main_result: The study finds that classical layers in hybrid architectures decouple trainability from expressibility, effectively eliminating the trade-off observed in pure PQCs under full end-to-end training.
method: A two-stage approach involving a systematic empirical analysis of gradient variance and KL divergence across various architectures, followed by a multi-objective Neural Architecture Search (NAS) using the NSGA-II algorithm.
model_or_system: Hybrid Quantum-Classical Neural Networks (HQNNs) consisting of classical convolutional/fully connected layers integrated with Parameterized Quantum Circuits (PQCs) using various entanglement topologies and rotation gates.
key_observables: Expressibility (via KL divergence), Trainability (via gradient variance), and Task Performance (via MNIST validation accuracy).
important_parameters: Circuit depth (L), qubit count (n), entanglement topologies (linear, star, all-to-all, etc.), and training configurations (pure PQC, quantum-only, and full end-to-end).
main_assumptions: Assumes that the properties of standalone PQCs might be used as design heuristics for hybrid models, a premise the paper seeks to challenge.
figures_summary: Figure 1 outlines the methodology and NAS framework; Figure 4 shows the weakening/elimination of the expressibility-trainability trade-off across different architectural settings; Figure 5 displays the Pareto-optimal fronts for the NAS results.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of the expressibility-trainability trade-off, presents a two-stage methodology (empirical analysis and NAS), details the experimental setup across different training regimes, reports findings on the decoupling of trade-offs, and concludes with the results of the automated architecture search.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Overview of the our two-stage methodology. Stage I presents the empirical analysis of the expressibility-trainability relationship in HQNNs. A flexible PQC design space is explored under multiple controlled settings and training configurations, including pure PQCs, full hybrid training, and quantum-only training. Expressibility (via KL divergence) and trainability (via gradient variance) are evaluated, revealing no consistent relationship between the two in hybrid models. Stage II introduces a multi-objective NAS framework over a joint classical-quantum design space. Architectures are optimized with respect to expressibility, trainability, and task performance using NSGA-II,...
Fig. 2: Synthetic dataset used for Expressibility-Trainability analysis of HQNNs
Fig. 3: Entanglement topologies used in this paper

Fig. 4: Stage I results across four architectural settings: (a) varying depth, (b) varying entanglement topology, (c) varying number of qubits, and (d) all parameters varied. Subplots (x1), (x2), and (x3) correspond to standalone PQCs, hybrid quantum-only training, and hybrid full-model training, respectively. −log10(gradvar) is based in Eq. 10, where smallers values means high gradiant variance (Better trainability) and vice versa. KL-Divergence is based on Eq. 11, where lower value means high expressibility and versa. The results show that the expressibility–trainability relationship observed in PQCs significantly weakens or breaks down in hybrid models with frozen classical layers, and...

Fig. 5: NAS results for HQNN architectures under two trainability definitions. The blue panels correspond to full-model trainability (gradients computed with respect to all model parameters), while the orange panels correspond to quantum-only trainability (gradients computed with respect to quantum parameters only in hybrid setting). Each point represents a candidate architecture, and red stars indicate Pareto-optimal solutions. The plots show the trade-offs between validation accuracy, expressibility, and trainability.
